text: . In this paper we introduce a new characterization of the NP complexity class called Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs) that is a natural extension of span programs introduced by Karchmer and Wigderson. Our motivation for introducing the class of QSPs is in order to design very quick protocols for the construction of “evidence” or “proofs” in the form of NIZK (non-interactive zero-knowledge) arguments for NP statements. Toward this end, our approach to the arithmetization of Boolean circuits is significantly diﬀerent from earlier approaches. Our approach allows for very dramatic improvements in the resulting constructions. Our main result here is a NIZK argument for Circuit-SAT in the common reference string (CRS) model based on QSPs that uses only 7 group elements. In addition, both the CRS length and the prover’s computation is quasi-linear, which is quite fast in practice (as we confirm with experimental results). The resulting NIZK argument has the shortest length of proofs that we know of, fastest prover and fastest verifier for Circuit-SAT. We also introduce a simple extension of the class of QSPs called Quadratic Arithmetic Programs (QAPs) that gives us a convenient way of describing arithmetic circuits over large fields. In addition, we obtain a new family of NIZK proof protocols that are based on these circuits and are very simple to prove correct, while still remaining eﬃcient. We use both our characterizations of NP in order to design very eﬃcient public protocols for publicly verifiable computation. Specifically, we show that QSPs and the QAPs can be used in order to publicly verify that a server that is given some input F has computed the output of an arbitrary polynomial time function of that input. That is, for any (possibly randomized) function F of an input x and for any polynomial time prover that receives the output of F as input, our server protocol allows for the verification of the correctness of the computation performed on F by the server in constant time which is less than the time required in order to evaluate F given the input x. In addition, the server’s computation time is constant which is also an improvement compared to the required time in order to evaluate F.
explanation: Reasonably technical and coherent, the passage resembles cryptography literature and could be human-written. However, some phrasing choices and emphatic claims (e.g., 'uses only 7 group elements') are reminiscent of AI-generated text.
